Arguments.prompt: Download US state boundary shapefiles from the Census Bureau at https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_state_20m.zip and US county boundary shapefiles from https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2018/shp/cb_2018_us_county_5m.zip. Each URL is a ZIP archive containing a shapefile set; download each ZIP, extract it in memory or to a temporary directory, and load the shapefile using geopandas. From the state shapefile, filter to the single feature where the NAME field equals 'Oregon' and save it as a GeoDataFrame with CRS EPSG:4326. From the county shapefile, filter to the single feature where the NAME field equals 'Benton' and the STATEFP field matches Oregon's STATEFP value (which is '41'). Ensure both GeoDataFrames use CRS EPSG:4326. Compute the bounding box of the Benton County geometry: obtain minx, miny, maxx, maxy from the geometry's total_bounds. Expand this bounding box by 0.5 degrees in each direction: expanded_minx = minx - 0.5, expanded_miny = miny - 0.5, expanded_maxx = maxx + 0.5, expanded_maxy = maxy + 0.5. Create a GeoDataFrame containing a single rectangular polygon representing this expanded bounding box using shapely.geometry.box(expanded_minx, expanded_miny, expanded_maxx, expanded_maxy) with CRS EPSG:4326. Save the Oregon state geometry as WeatherGeographicPlot/State.geojson, the Benton County geometry as WeatherGeographicPlot/County.geojson, and the expanded bounding box polygon as WeatherGeographicPlot/OuterBoundary.geojson. Also save the four bounding box coordinate values as a JSON file WeatherGeographicPlot/BoundingBox.json with keys expanded_minx, expanded_miny, expanded_maxx, expanded_maxy. Upload all five files (State.geojson, County.geojson, OuterBoundary.geojson, BoundingBox.json) to the S3 data store under the folder WeatherGeographicPlot.

Download WeatherGeographicPlot/BoundingBox.json from the S3 data store. This file contains keys expanded_minx, expanded_miny, expanded_maxx, expanded_maxy representing the expanded bounding box around Benton County, Oregon. Download the NOAA GHCND station inventory from https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/pub/data/ghcn/daily/ghcnd-inventory.txt. This is a fixed-width text file with no header. Each line has the following columns in fixed-width format: STATION (characters 1-11, 0-indexed columns 0:11), LATITUDE (columns 12:20), LONGITUDE (columns 21:30), ELEMENT (columns 31:35), FIRSTYEAR (columns 36:40), LASTYEAR (columns 41:45). Parse the file using Python's string slicing or pandas read_fwf with widths [11, 9, 10, 5, 5, 5] and column names ['STATION', 'LATITUDE', 'LONGITUDE', 'ELEMENT', 'FIRSTYEAR', 'LASTYEAR']. Convert LATITUDE and LONGITUDE to float, FIRSTYEAR and LASTYEAR to int. Determine the current year using datetime.datetime.today().year. Filter rows to only those where ELEMENT is 'TMAX' or 'TMIN' and LASTYEAR is greater than or equal to the current year. For each station, it must have both a TMAX record and a TMIN record that are active (LASTYEAR >= current year). Group by STATION and keep only stations that have at least one active TMAX row and at least one active TMIN row. Then filter those stations to only those where LATITUDE is between expanded_miny and expanded_maxy (inclusive) and LONGITUDE is between expanded_minx and expanded_maxx (inclusive). Build a GeoDataFrame from the filtered stations with one row per station, using the station's LATITUDE and LONGITUDE to create a Point geometry (longitude, latitude), with CRS EPSG:4326. Include columns STATION, LATITUDE, LONGITUDE in the GeoDataFrame. Save the result as WeatherGeographicPlot/Stations.geojson and upload it to the S3 data store under the folder WeatherGeographicPlot.

Download WeatherGeographicPlot/Stations.geojson from the S3 data store. This GeoJSON file contains a GeoDataFrame with columns STATION, LATITUDE, LONGITUDE, and geometry (Point). Determine the target week as follows: take today's date using datetime.date.today(), subtract 28 days to get a reference date, then find the most recent Monday on or before that reference date by subtracting (reference_date.weekday()) days (where Monday=0). This Monday is the week_start. The week_end is week_start plus 6 days (the following Sunday). For each station in the GeoDataFrame, download its full daily CSV record from the URL https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/data/global-historical-climatology-network-daily/access/<STATION_ID>.csv where <STATION_ID> is the value in the STATION column. The CSV has a header row. Relevant columns are: DATE (format YYYY-MM-DD), TMIN, TMAX. Parse the DATE column as datetime.date objects. Filter rows to only those where DATE is between week_start and week_end inclusive. Drop any rows where TMIN or TMAX is missing (NaN or empty). If after filtering a station has no valid rows, skip that station entirely. For stations with valid data, compute the mean of TMIN across all remaining rows and the mean of TMAX across all remaining rows. Divide both means by 10.0 to convert from tenths of a degree Celsius to whole degrees Celsius. Collect results into a list of records with keys STATION, TMIN_avg, TMAX_avg, LATITUDE, LONGITUDE, and geometry (the Point geometry from the input GeoDataFrame). Build a GeoDataFrame from these records with CRS EPSG:4326 and columns STATION, TMIN, TMAX (using TMIN_avg and TMAX_avg as the column values), LATITUDE, LONGITUDE, geometry. Also save the week_start and week_end dates as a JSON file WeatherGeographicPlot/WeekDates.json with keys week_start (format YYYY-MM-DD) and week_end (format YYYY-MM-DD). Save the temperature GeoDataFrame as WeatherGeographicPlot/TemperatureData.geojson and upload both WeatherGeographicPlot/TemperatureData.geojson and WeatherGeographicPlot/WeekDates.json to the S3 data store under the folder WeatherGeographicPlot.

Download the following files from the S3 data store folder WeatherGeographicPlot: OuterBoundary.geojson, TemperatureData.geojson, State.geojson, County.geojson, BoundingBox.json, and WeekDates.json. Load each GeoJSON file using geopandas.read_file(). Load BoundingBox.json as a Python dict with keys expanded_minx, expanded_miny, expanded_maxx, expanded_maxy (all floats). Load WeekDates.json as a Python dict with keys week_start and week_end (strings in YYYY-MM-DD format); parse them as datetime.date objects. From TemperatureData.geojson, extract station longitudes as an array (from geometry.x), station latitudes as an array (from geometry.y), TMIN values as a float array, and TMAX values as a float array. Build a 100x100 interpolation grid: grid_lon = numpy.linspace(expanded_minx, expanded_maxx, 100) and grid_lat = numpy.linspace(expanded_miny, expanded_maxy, 100), then grid_lon_2d, grid_lat_2d = numpy.meshgrid(grid_lon, grid_lat). Use scipy.interpolate.griddata with method='cubic' to interpolate TMIN values onto the grid: tmin_grid = scipy.interpolate.griddata((station_lons, station_lats), tmin_values, (grid_lon_2d, grid_lat_2d), method='cubic'). Similarly interpolate TMAX: tmax_grid = scipy.interpolate.griddata((station_lons, station_lats), tmax_values, (grid_lon_2d, grid_lat_2d), method='cubic'). Create a matplotlib figure with two side-by-side subplots using fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 7)). For the left subplot (ax1, TMIN): use ax1.contourf(grid_lon_2d, grid_lat_2d, tmin_grid, levels=20, cmap='Blues_r', alpha=0.8). Add a colorbar to ax1 labeled 'Temperature (°C)'. Overlay station scatter points using ax1.scatter(station_lons, station_lats, c=tmin_values, cmap='Blues_r', edgecolors='black', zorder=5, s=60). Draw the Oregon state boundary on ax1 using State.geojson: iterate over its geometries and plot each polygon boundary as a black line with linewidth=1.5. Draw the Benton County boundary on ax1 using County.geojson: iterate over its geometries and plot each polygon boundary as a dark red line with linewidth=2.0. Set ax1.set_xlim(expanded_minx, expanded_maxx) and ax1.set_ylim(expanded_miny, expanded_maxy). Set ax1.set_aspect('equal'). Set x-axis ticks at intervals of 0.5 degrees between expanded_minx and expanded_maxx using numpy.arange. Set y-axis ticks at intervals of 0.5 degrees between expanded_miny and expanded_maxy. Label ax1 x-axis as 'Longitude' and y-axis as 'Latitude'. Title ax1 as 'Minimum Temperature Heatmap (°C)'. For the right subplot (ax2, TMAX): use ax2.contourf(grid_lon_2d, grid_lat_2d, tmax_grid, levels=20, cmap='Reds', alpha=0.8). Add a colorbar to ax2 labeled 'Temperature (°C)'. Overlay station scatter points using ax2.scatter(station_lons, station_lats, c=tmax_values, cmap='Reds', edgecolors='black', zorder=5, s=60). Draw the Oregon state boundary on ax2 using State.geojson the same way as ax1. Draw the Benton County boundary on ax2 using County.geojson the same way as ax1. Set ax2.set_xlim(expanded_minx, expanded_maxx) and ax2.set_ylim(expanded_miny, expanded_maxy). Set ax2.set_aspect('equal'). Set x-axis and y-axis ticks at 0.5-degree intervals the same as ax1. Label ax2 x-axis as 'Longitude' and y-axis as 'Latitude'. Title ax2 as 'Maximum Temperature Heatmap (°C)'. Format the figure supertitle as follows: format week_start as a string like 'Monday, January 6, 2025' using strftime('%A, %B %-d, %Y') (or equivalent platform-safe formatting), and format week_end similarly. Set the figure supertitle using fig.suptitle(f'Temperature Heatmap for Benton County, Oregon\n{week_start_str} through {week_end_str}', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold'). Call plt.tight_layout() and save the figure as WeatherGeographicPlot/TemperatureHeatmap.png using plt.savefig with dpi=150 and bbox_inches='tight'. Upload WeatherGeographicPlot/TemperatureHeatmap.png to the S3 data store under the folder WeatherGeographicPlot.
